episodes.title: I Watched 15 Years’ Worth of Epstein Police Footage. Here’s What They Knew.
episodes.description:
Buried deep within millions of pages of the recently released Epstein files are hundreds of hours of hard-to-obtain footage.

Earlier this month, we published all 14 hours of the footage from Data Set 10, a raft of videos seized from Epstein’s electronic devices. The videos painted the most vivid picture yet of Epstein’s dark world.

Today, we are publishing 12 hours of footage from Data Set 9. This collection traces more than a decade of efforts by law enforcement to investigate Epstein. There are tapes of police searching his mansion, clips from depositions spanning multiple years and cases, and even covert footage of a Federal Bureau of Investigation sting operation to retrieve his infamous “little black book.” The footage shows, on camera, a game of cat and mouse between Epstein and law enforcement that lasted more than a decade. One way to think about it is that Data Set 10 captured Epstein’s world as he saw it, while Data Set 9 captures authorities’ long, uneven effort to dismantle it.

Of all 12 batches of material that were released by the Department of Justice last month, the footage from Data Set 9 was the hardest to obtain. That’s because in addition to the structural obstacles in the agency’s website—such as no master index and no ability to browse or search by file type—Data Set 9 was partially taken down shortly after its release, reportedly following complaints that some files contained unredacted child pornography. As a result, the bulk download link was disabled, which means that even anyone willing to download hundreds of gigabytes of data was no longer able to do so.

The Free Press cross-checked multiple online repositories, which claim to host full or partial versions of the archive, to account for all of the currently available video files in Data Set 9. We are publishing this footage in its entirety, excluding only obvious duplicates and extensive prison closed-circuit television recordings. (Those duplicates and recordings are linked to in the show notes.)

Numerous images that line the walls in Epstein’s mansion in Palm Beach, Florida, recorded by police during a search, depict fully or partially naked young females—some of whom might be among Epstein’s alleged victims. We decided to keep those images as is in the full 12-hour version of Data Set 9 for consistency with how the Department of Justice released them. But we are blurring all of the sensitive imagery in a shorter video that summarizes the full footage.

Data Set 9 also includes a handful of what appear to be personal videos seized from Epstein’s devices. There are short clips of Epstein with notable figures such as Woody Allen, Bill Gates, and former Norwegian diplomat Terje Rød-Larsen. But those clips are few and far between. Most of the footage was captured by authorities trying to bring Epstein to justice through investigations that ranged from local to federal and spanned multiple agencies, states, and jurisdictions.

Watched together, these videos—an hourlong house tour, the FBI’s recovery of key evidence, depositions, and testimonies from Epstein’s enablers and staff—demonstrate just how much was meticulously documented about Epstein’s life and conduct after law enforcement began looking into him in 2005.

One of the most important questions about the Epstein case is this: Why didn’t Epstein face more serious legal repercussions sooner? This collection of videos offers no conclusive answer, but it does make the question harder to ignore.

episodes.title: Epstein Police Footage: Inside His Mansion, FBI Sting, Jail Cell & More | Part 2
episodes.description:
On January 30, 2026, the Department of Justice released more than three million pages of material related to Jeffrey Epstein.

But very few of the more than 2,000 video files included in the release have been seen by the public. That’s because they are stored in a way that makes them very hard to find or browse.

The Free Press wanted to make this archive more easily viewable for the public, so viewers can understand this story for themselves.

We previously published hours of footage from Data Set 10: footage seized from Epstein’s electronic devices that offered a glimpse into his private world.

We are now publishing the full 12 hours of footage from Data Set 9, excluding only obvious duplicates and extensive CCTV prison footage, which we will link to in the show notes.

This data set contains hundreds of videos, generated over more than a decade of law enforcement investigation of Jeffrey Epstein.

This is the fourth installment of the Epstein Tapes.

Viewer discretion is advised.

We excluded from this recording extensive hours of CCTV prison footage. To watch those yourself, you can follow these directions:

All currently available prison CCTV recordings are listed by their unique EFTA  log number, which can be found at the very end of individual DOJ links, i.e.:

http://justice.gov/epstein/files/DataSet%209/EFTA00064598.mp4

To view each file, copy and paste the beginning of the link to your browser:
http://justice.gov/epstein/files/DataSet%209/

Then add the file name you want to view directly after it. Ensure there are no spaces, and that the end of the link is a file extension (.mp4)

The "--" in the list denotes that the numbers go in chronological order, and that the file exists for each (i.e. EFTA00064598.mp4 -- EFTA00064614.mp4 means that there are 17 consecutive files from EFTA00064598 all the way to EFTA00064614)

EFTA00064598.mp4 -- EFTA00064614.mp4
EFTA00099787.mp4
EFTA00099789.mp4
EFTA00108920.mp4 -- EFTA00108932.mp4
EFTA00128448.mp4 -- EFTA00128626.mp4
EFTA00137534.mp4 -- EFTA00137571.mp4
EFTA00164926.mp4

episodes.title: Jeffrey Epstein’s Twisted Obsession with ‘Lolita’
episodes.description:
One particular novel is all over the Epstein files: Vladimir Nabokov’s “Lolita.”

Among the more than three million files related to Jeffrey Epstein that were released three weeks ago are scores of references to the book. “Lolita” pops up in emails and in photos, released by the House Committee on Oversight, that show young women with quotes from the book written on their bodies.

Reportedly, this was the one and only book Epstein kept at his bedside table. He owned a first edition. He was a massive fan of Nabokov. And, of course, his plane was famously nicknamed the “Lolita Express.”

“Lolita” is a book about a 37-year-old man who kidnaps and serially rapes a 12-year-old girl. There has long been a fascination with “Lolita” in American pop culture. The illicit relationship it depicts is often glamorized in film, music, and art.

Today, Rafaela Siewert interviews Shilo Brooks about “Lolita”—and how a novel about a homicidal pedophile rapist came to occupy such a prominent place in American life. You can watch the full episode below or listen on Apple, Spotify, or wherever you get your podcasts.

0:00-Live from the Comedy Cellar
0:54-From Evangelical Convert to Public Skeptic
4:58- Personal Experience vs. Verifiable Reality
7:14-If Religion Makes People Happier, Why Choose Truth?
9:20-Can Secular Society Replace the Church?
12:48-Jesus: History, Myth, and Resurrection Claims
17:35-Why Bother Debunking Conspiracy Theories?
21:06-How to Change a Conspiracy Theorist’s Mind
22:45-Real Conspiracies vs. Fantastical Ones
25:23-Are Conspiracies Rising on the Right?
28:43-The Personality of the Conspiracy Mind
29:59-Atlantis and the Allure of Lost Civilizations
31:53-UFOs: Aliens Out There vs. Aliens Here
36:05-Science in Crisis: Fraud, Replication, and Trust
43:50-Uncertainty, COVID, and Public Trust
46:16-Which Political Side Is More Anti-Science?
50:48-Risk, Endurance, and the Skeptic’s Temperament
53:52-Hallucinations, Near-Death Experiences, and Belief

episodes.title: Previously Buried Ghislaine Maxwell Testimony | The Epstein Tapes
episodes.description:
Buried among the hundreds of videos in the latest release of the Epstein files is the footage of Jeffrey Epstein’s most notorious accomplice and the only person alive who knows the true extent of his crimes: Ghislaine Maxwell. 

These clips are from Maxwell’s 2016 deposition, part of a civil case brought by Virginia Giuffre, who accused Maxwell and Epstein of running a sex-trafficking operation that abused her when she was a minor. 

The Free Press is publishing the newly released clips in chronological order.

episodes.title: The Complete Epstein Tapes | Dataset 10 | Part 2
episodes.description:
On January 30, 2026, the DOJ released more than three million pages of material related to Jeffrey Epstein. But very few of the more than 2,000 video files included in the release have been seen by the public. That’s because they are stored in a way that makes them very hard to find or browse.

The Free Press wanted to make this archive more easily viewable for the public, so viewers can understand this story for themselves.

The Free Press downloaded the most important set of video files:

Data Set 10.

It contains the majority of the video files in the release, and we are publishing the entirety of the 14 hours of footage, excluding only obvious duplicates. These are the Epstein Tapes. Viewer discretion is advised.

All redactions were done by the DOJ.

episodes.title: I Watched 14 Hours of Epstein Videos. Here’s What I Saw.
episodes.description:
On January 30, 2026, the Department of Justice released more than three million files related to Jeffrey Epstein. And buried within that are roughly 2,000 previously unpublished videos. But while they’re technically public, they are stored in a way that makes them very hard to find or browse. 
To watch these videos, the public has two choices: download hundreds of gigabytes of data— or click through hundreds of web pages, one by one.

The Free Press's Tanya Lukyanova did both. 

The vast majority of videos—and by far the most revealing ones—are in Data Set 10. These are videos seized from Epstein’s devices: footage he recorded himself, received from others, or downloaded from the internet.

All redactions were done by the DOJ. Viewer discretion is advised.

The DOJ dumped 14 hours of video into the Epstein files. For the first time, The Free Press put them all in one place.

Watch Part 1: https://www.thefp.com/p/watch-the-jeffrey-epstein-tapes-part
Watch Part 2: https://www.thefp.com/p/watch-the-epstein-tapes-part-2

Support independent journalism. Subscribe to The Free Press today: https://www.thefp.com/subscribe.

episodes.title: Did Trump’s Allies Help Break Andrew Tate Out of Romania?
episodes.description:
“Reject weakness in any form.” 

“The world is hypercompetitive. If you’re going to be a man who’s going to sit and say, ‘I’m just sad,’ you are always going to lose in competition to men like me.”

“My sister is her husband’s property.”

“There is no such thing as an independent female.”

This is all Andrew Tate, the controversial “manosphere” influencer, whose content has racked up billions of views online. His blend of self-help rhetoric and shock-value provocation has earned him notoriety, millions of followers, and millions of dollars.

In the 2010s, Andrew rose to prominence alongside his brother, Tristan, through viral clips on TikTok, X, and YouTube, preaching ideas about how to make money, build physical discipline, and become a “high-value man.” He did this while also running a webcam porn business, which involved getting women to perform on camera for paying customers. And he sold courses teaching other men how to build similar operations—a course he dubbed PHD, for “pimping hoes degree.”

In December 2022, the brothers were arrested in Romania. The subsequent investigation alleged that they trafficked dozens of women. Andrew was also accused of rape and having sex with a minor. Romanian authorities blocked the brothers from leaving the country while the investigation was underway. But in February 2025, the ban was lifted—with little explanation—allowing the Tates to make their way back to the U.S.

Megan Twohey is an investigative journalist at The New York Times. Twohey and Isabella Kwai just published a major investigation into how the Tate brothers sought to forge relationships with right-wing influencers and members of the Trump family—and whether those connections played a role in their travel ban being lifted.

I ask Twohey how Andrew Tate—a former kickboxer—became one of the internet’s most polarizing figures; I ask about the accusations against Andrew and Tristan Tate spanning three countries; about Andrew’s reported relationships with Donald Trump Jr. and Barron Trump; about the meetings between Trump envoy Richard Grenell and Romanian officials that may have paved the way for their return to the United States; and how the Tates—and their lifted travel ban—divided the right.

It’s a conversation you won’t want to miss.

episodes.title: Inside OneTaste: The Wellness Trend That Hid a Sex Cult
episodes.description:
It’s not every day we witness the downfall of a pro-orgasm wellness guru. But in June 2025, a federal jury sent founder—and alleged cult leader—Nicole Daedone to jail.

Two decades earlier, Daedone had founded OneTaste, a global wellness company promising sexual education, empowerment, and even enlightenment. 

The company’s signature offering was a 15-minute clitoral-stroking method called “orgasmic meditation,” or OM, supplemented by for-purchase demonstrations, workshops, and retreats for members to immerse themselves in the practice. The goal was to make OM as common as yoga or meditation. “Do you want to OM?” was meant to reverberate across the San Francisco wellness community. 

At its peak, OneTaste drew endorsements from Gwyneth Paltrow, Khloé Kardashian, Theo Von, and Tim Ferriss. But behind closed doors, a very different reality was taking shape—one that former members say involved exploitation, labor violations, and sexual trauma. Some have even called it a “prostitution ring.”

Bloomberg journalist Ellen Huet spent six years investigating OneTaste and broke the first story about its abuse. She chronicles the rise and collapse in her new book, Empire of Orgasm: Sex, Power, and the Downfall of a Wellness Cult.

I ask her what really happened inside the organization; how to understand consent and agency in a cult environment; how the movement took on religious overtones; and how, in the end, this “wellness revolution” left so many lives in ruins.
